text: A fifty-six-year-old obese man with bilateral inguinal hernia came to my observation with a strange symptom.
After a mixturation the scrotal swelling decreased in size.
To physical examination, the scrotum hernia seemed irreducible, so that the patient was subjected to an abdominal and pelvis TC.
Emanation showed the presence of a right scrotal systole cell, a large sliding inguinal hernia involving the urinary bladder.
Since the bladder is an extraperitoneal organ, the extraperitoneal approach has seemed to be the most appropriate.
The surgeon creates the preperitoneal space and inserts two to five millimeter trocars into the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels, which are visible as anatomical landmarks and appear detached from the abdominal wall.
The video shows a subverted anatomy in the area, likely due to the presence of a large sliding inguinal hernia involving the urinary bladder, as previously noted.
The surgeon delicately dissects and liberates the hernia sac to reduce it, while beginning to identify the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the bladder, which is visibly not covered by the peritoneal sac.
According to the classification of the scrotal cystosale depending on the relationship with the peritoneum, it can be defined as extraperitoneal.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the bladder through the hernia orifice, encountering difficulty as the bladder is manipulated within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the hernia as an external oblique hernia, located lateral to the epigastric vessels, with visible anatomical distortion in the area.
After reducing the bladder, the surgeon reveals clearer anatomical structures within the surgical field, facilitating further identification and dissection.
The surgeon highlights the funicular structures, revealing another hernia adjacent to the previously dissected area.
The surgeon dissects and carefully isolates the sac of the second oblique external hernia, progressively reducing it.
It's essential always to identify the various structures of the core to avoid the risk of having recurrences.
The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical site to assess the area for the placement of the prosthesis.
The surgeon sections the remaining adhesions in the direction of the bladder and the spermatic cord, further clarifying the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts a macroporous partially absorbable lightweight mesh into the extraperitoneal space and secures it to Cooper's ligament using absorbable tags.
Routine time was one hundred minutes.
No blood loss and discharge was in the first post-operative day.
The patient has achieved immediate resolution of the symptoms after surgery and there is no recurrence at five months of follow-up.
